title: The White Savior Film: Content, Critics, and Consumption By Matthew W. Hughey Temple University Press. 2014. 230 pages. $29.95 paper, $89.50 cloth
abstract: Two features of Hughey's study immediately distinguish it from the plethora of volumes produced today under the Film Studies rubric. The first is that it is systematically analytic and empirical. The second is that those methodological commitments are placed in the service of a distinctively sociological approach to his chosen topic. That topic, of course, is also of great interest in its own right, and the fact that he is examining white savior films as a sociologist rather than as a film critic makes his a distinctive and valuable contribution both to studying cinema and to understanding the ideological formation of race in contemporary American society. The book's subtitle—Content, Critics, and Consumption—is a succinct summary of its structure. After a contextualizing introduction, three substantial chapters offer respectively a content analysis of a sample of 50 white savior films produced between 1987 and 2011, a systematic account of critical comment on those films in 2,799 North American–based reviews, and an analysis of responses from 83 viewers of three selected films, via focus group and interview. In each case, Hughey employs the language of “frames” and “themes” to explore the systems of meaning at work in the film texts, in the film reviewers’ interpretive community, and among his range of focus-group and interview subjects. Informing the argument at every stage is a view of contemporary racial discourse as “an insidious form of racial neoliberalism that rationalizes inequality and discrimination, not through collective appeals to racial domination or racial consciousness, but through appeals to individualism and color blindness” (78).
